title: Quiz: Quantum Amplitude Estimation
description: Test your understanding of quantum amplitude estimation algorithms

questions.question: What is the query complexity of canonical quantum amplitude estimation to achieve precision epsilon?
questions.explanation: Quantum amplitude estimation achieves O(1/epsilon) query complexity, which is a quadratic speedup compared to classical Monte Carlo sampling which requires O(1/epsilon^2) samples.

questions.question: What are the eigenvalues of the Grover operator Q when the target amplitude is a = sin^2(theta)?
questions.explanation: The Grover operator has eigenvalues e^(+/- 2i*theta) where sin^2(theta) = a. This is why quantum phase estimation can be used to estimate theta and thus a.

questions.question: Why does canonical amplitude estimation require ancilla qubits?

options.text: To store the state preparation operator
To measure in the computational basis

questions.explanation: Canonical QAE uses quantum phase estimation to extract the phase theta from the Grover operator's eigenvalues. QPE requires ancilla qubits to store the phase estimate in binary.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Maximum Likelihood Amplitude Estimation (MLAE) over canonical QAE?

options.text: It uses flexible circuit depths without ancillas
It provides exact answers with probability 1

questions.explanation: MLAE allows flexible circuit depths and doesn't require ancilla qubits for phase estimation. This makes it more suitable for near-term quantum devices with limited coherence times.

questions.question: The amplitude estimation algorithm can only estimate probabilities, not expectation values of arbitrary observables.
questions.explanation: False. While QAE directly estimates amplitudes (probabilities), it can be used to estimate expectation values by encoding the observable into the state preparation. This is the basis of quantum Monte Carlo methods.

questions.question: In the amplitude estimation problem, if A|0> = sqrt(1-a)|psi_0>|0> + sqrt(a)|psi_1>|1>, what does 'a' represent?

options.text: The amplitude of the ground state
The probability of measuring the marked/good state
The phase of the quantum state
The fidelity with the target state

questions.explanation: The parameter 'a' represents the probability of measuring the marked or 'good' state (indicated by the |1> flag). This is the squared amplitude sqrt(a)^2 = a.

questions.question: How many precision qubits are approximately needed in canonical QAE to achieve precision epsilon = 0.01?
questions.explanation: The number of precision qubits needed is approximately log_2(pi/epsilon). For epsilon = 0.01, this is log_2(314) ≈ 8.3, so about 9 qubits are needed.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid applications of quantum amplitude estimation? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quantum counting - counting solutions to search problems

questions.explanation: QAE is used in quantum counting (to count search solutions), option pricing (financial derivatives), and quantum Monte Carlo (expectation estimation). Shor's algorithm uses phase estimation but not amplitude estimation per se.
